paper_title: Understanding the Behaviors of Environment-aware Information Retrieval

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches have demonstrated strong capability in handling complex queries, yet current research overlooks a critical challenge: different retrievers require fundamentally different query formulation strategies for optimal performance. In this work, we present the first systematic analysis of how LLMs can learn to adapt their query formulation strategies for different retrievers via reinforcement learning (RL). Our empirical study reveals that RL effectively teaches an LLM to tailor its queries to specific retriever characteristics. We discover that different retrievers exhibit surprisingly distinct optimal query styles (e.g., descriptive vs. question-like), suggesting strategies learned for one retriever ineffective for another. We further show that performance can be enhanced by incorporating retriever-specific human guidance and by scaling model size. To facilitate learning over multi-retrieval-step trajectories, we introduce a branching-based rollout technique that improves training stability. Our work provides the first empirical evidence and actionable insights for building truly retriever-aware RAG systems. Code and resources are available at https://github.com/LCO-Embedding/Envs-aware-Information-Retrieval.

However, "right now" might be bit too specific or could be interpreted as timerelated keyword. Maybe replacing "right now" with "current" or "now" could be better. Also, using "current team" instead of "play for right now" might be more effective. {... sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) approaches have demonstrated strong capability in handling complex queries, yet current research overlooks critical challenge: different retrievers require fundamentally different query formulation strategies for optimal performance. In this work, we present the first systematic analysis of how LLMs can learn *Equal Contribution. Corresponding authors, xiaochenghao@sufe.edu.cn kenchanhp@gmail.com, to adapt their query formulation strategies for different retrievers via reinforcement learning (RL). Our empirical study reveals that RL effectively teaches an LLM to tailor its queries to specific retriever characteristics. We discover that different retrievers exhibit surprisingly distinct optimal query styles (e.g., descriptive vs. question-like), suggesting strategies learned for one retriever ineffective for another. We further show that performance can be enhanced by incorporating retriever-specific human guidance and by scaling model size. To facilitate learning over multi-retrieval-step trajectories, we introduce branching-based rollout technique that improves training stability. Our work provides the first empirical evidence and actionable insights for building truly retriever-aware RAG systems. Code and resources are available at LCO-Embedding/Envs-aware-InformationRetrieval.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is standard approach for augmenting the knowledge of large language models (LLMs) by generating queries and retrieving relevant information from external sources. Recently, Agentic RAG has emerged as more sophisticated paradigm that, rather than performing single retrieval step, decomposes complex queries and iteratively executes multiple retrieval operations to fulfill complex task requirements, such as generating comprehensive research reports in real-world applications. Most agentic RAG approaches treat retrieval as uniform tool call, operating under the assumption that it occurs exclusively through single commercial search engine (e.g., Google) and optimizing for that specific engine. However, retrieval from locally stored information repositories remains critically important in real-world applications, including private financial reports and similar patient case databases. In practice, agentic RAG systems must accommodate diverse retrieval backends. Different retrievers may require distinct optimal query formulation strategies to maximize retrieval effectivenessan LLM should fundamentally adapt how it formulates queries depending on which retriever it is querying. However, current research lacks adequate investigation into how LLMs can develop retriever-aware query formulation capabilities. In this work, we present the first systematic analysis of how LLMs learn to adapt their query formulation strategies for different retrievers via reinforcement learning (RL). We systematically examine the effects of different retriever types, the comprehensiveness of human guidance in prompts, and the scaling of model size and retrieval steps, via the following research questions. RQ1: Can LLMs adapt their query formulation strategies to local retrievers via RL? If so, are strategies learned for one retriever transferable to others? We find that RL effectively adapts an LLMs query formulation strategy to local retrievers. Moreover, we discover that different retrievers exhibit distinct optimal query formulation strategies. For example, Contriever (Izacard et al., 2022) requires long-form descriptive queries while Qwen3-Embedding (Zhang et al., 2025) performs better with question-like queries. Consequently, strategies optimized for one retriever cannot be effectively transferred to different retrievers. RQ2: How does the comprehensiveness of human guidance in prompts affect the learning of query formulation? We design three levels of knowledge comprehensiveness in human guidance: from general query rewriting strategies to retriever-specific query formulation by human experts. We find that more comprehensive retrieverspecific knowledge in prompts leads to better LLM performance, suggesting that LLMs benefit from human expertise to guide RL exploration in query formulation. RQ3: Can we enhance query formulation performance by scaling up model size and the number of retrieval steps? We find that larger models achieve better performance and can even discover policies that human experts do not anticipate. Moreover, to support multi-step retrieval trajectories during rollouts, we introduce branching technique that isolates the learning signal to the final turn. Experimental results show that increasing the number of retrieval steps enhances query formulation capability, as the model can leverage documents retrieved in earlier steps to refine subsequent query formulation strategies. We summarize our key contribution as follows: (1) We present the first systematic empirical study analyzing how LLMs learn to adapt their query formulation strategies for different retrievers via reinforcement learning. (2) Our results reveal distinct optimal query formulation strategies for prominent retrievers, providing actionable insights for RAG system practitioners. (3) We introduce branchingbased optimization technique to support multi-step retrieval trajectory rollouts, facilitating stable RL training for multi-step query strategy optimization.

sections.title: 2 Methodology
sections.content: We formulate retriever-aware query adaptation as reinforcement learning (RL) problem, in which an LLMbased query rewriter policy πθ learns to generate optimal search queries for given blackbox retriever environment E, conditioned on userissued question. The policy is optimized using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) (Shao et al., 2024), which enables stable learning without requiring value-function critic. 2.1 Reward Function Formulation critical component of our framework is the reward signal provided by the environment. For generated query q, the retriever returns set of documents D. We define the reward r(q, D) as the Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG@10) of the retrieved documents with respect to the ground-truth passage d. We select nDCG over simple recall to incentivize the model to not only find the correct document but to rank it highly, mirroring real-world RAG requirements. 2.2 Scenario 1: Single-Turn Retrieval In the single-turn setting, the policy generates rewrite given an input x. For each input, we sample group of outputs {q1, ..., qG} from the policy πθold. The GRPO objective is maximizing: LGRP O(θ) = 1 (cid:88) i=1 1 yi yi (cid:88) (cid:20) t=1 min (ρi,tAi, clip(ρi,t, 1 ϵ, 1 + ϵ)Ai) βDKL(πθπref ) (cid:21) , where ρi = πθ(qix) (1) (qix) is the importance sampling ratio. The advantage Ai is computed by normalizing the reward ri against the group statistics: πθold Ai = ri µgroup σgroup + δ , (2) where µgroup and σgroup are the mean and standard deviation of rewards within the group. 2.3 Scenario 2: Multi-Turn Iterative Retrieval To enable the agent to refine queries based on initial feedback, we define multi-turn trajectory τi = [qi,1, ei,1, qi,2, ei,2, ..., qi,K], where qi,k is the query (and reasoning) at turn k, and ei,k represents the search results (masked during training). Weighted Reward Aggregation. To value the final answer while encouraging useful intermediate steps, we compute the total trajectory reward Ri as weighted sum: Ri = (cid:88) k=1 λk r(qi,k, ei,k). (3) In practice, we assign higher weight to later rounds, which is discussed in implementation details and multi-round training techniques section. 2.4 Branching Rollout Strategy key challenge in multi-turn RL is the high variance of intermediate rewards, which leads to training collapse. Hence, we introduce Branching Rollout strategy that stabilizes credit assignment. In this work, we focus on two-turn RL setting. Instead of independent linear trajectories (e.g., 16 1), we implement branching structure (e.g., 4 4) where we sample first-turn actions, and for each, branch into second-turn completions. This yields two benefits: Monte Carlo Estimation for Turn 1: The value of first-turn action qi,1 is estimated by averaging over its branches, providing robust signal for the intermediate step: (qi,1) r(qi,1) + 1 (cid:88) j=1 r(q(j) i,2 ). (4) Variance Reduction for Turn 2: By grouping the branches that share the same history, the baseline (mean reward) becomes specific to that sub-group. This isolates the learning signal for the second turn, ensuring that the advantage calculation reflects the quality of the second rewrite independent of the first turns quality.

sections.title: 3 Experiments
sections.content: 3.1 Settings In-distribution Evaluation We leverage RAGBench (Friel et al., 2024), large-scale benchmark for evaluating retrieval-augmented generation systems. RAGBench comprises more than 100,000 samples, spans broad range of general and specialized domains, and exhibits substantial variation in document structure and information needs, which makes it well suited for comprehensive evaluation of model performance. To adapt RAGBench for retrieval tasks, we first aggregate all documents in the dataset into global retrieval corpus, and treat the questions as search queries and their associate contexts as the positive pair for retrieval. Additionally, for corpus de-duplication, we employ Qwen-Embedding-0.6B (Zhang et al., 2025) to encode the documents, retaining only one instance from pairs that share similarity greater than 0.99. OOD Evaluation To evaluate the transferability of LLM query rewriter trained on general-domain RAG datasets like RAGBench, we conduct zeroshot experiments to the commonly-used BEIR benchmark (Thakur et al., 2021). Domain-specific Transfer To evaluate how well retriever-aware query rewriters trained on generaldomain datasets generalize to challenging domainspecific environments, we conduct an in-depth case study in the financial domain. Although underexplored in existing retrieval benchmarks, finance is an ideal stress test for cross-domain transfer due to (1) its pronounced distribution shift from generaldomain corpora induced by domain-specific terminology (e.g., SG&A, currency fluctuation, gross margin), (2) the hybrid structure of financial documents that interleaves text with tabular content, and (3) the practical relevance of information retrieval in real-world workflows in finance. To this end, we construct financial retrieval environment leveraging FinAgentBench (Choi et al., 2025) and describe the process in detail in Appendix B. 3.2 Implementation Details Infrastructure We serve the retrieval environments (retrievers and pre-indexed corpus) as APIs using FastAPI and uvicorn. The retrieval environments are served on different nodes, isolated from the main GRPO training process, which secures no interference with the RL training. RL implementation For multi-turn search, we assign 0.5 weighting to the first-round retrieval and 1 to the second-sround retrieval. We apply masking to the search results returned by the retrievers, and only compute the loss of the reasoning and the rewritten queries. This is based on the principle of not punishing or rewarding model for texts it didnt generate. We further ablate whether it is more optimal to apply n2 rollouts to the full unconditional two-turn search trajectories, or rollouts to the second-turn rewriting conditioned on firstturn rewriting and search results. Retrievers We select four representative retrieval including Qwen3-Embedding (Zhang systems, et al., 2025), all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (SentenceTransformers, 2021), Contriever (Izacard et al., 2022), and BM25 (Robertson and Zaragoza, 2009). Qwen3-Embedding and all-MiniLM-L6-v2 are dense embedding models trained on large-scale supervised pairs, and they respectively represent decoder/high-capability/large and encoder/lowcapability/small models. Contriever is an unsupervised representation model trained on randomly cropped spans of Wikipedia article as positive pairs. Therefore, Contriever intuitively prefers retrieving documents usQuestion-to-Statement Rewriting Policy discovered by 14B agent under Contriever environment. User Question: Is it possible to be white and latino? assistant: <think> Okay, lets tackle this question. The user is asking if its possible to be both white and Latino. need to figure out the best way to rewrite the query for Contriever to get the most relevant documents. ( ) First, should understand what Contriever does. Its trained on Wikipedia articles, using two spans as positive pairs. So, the model is good at finding documents where two phrases are related. The original query is about the intersection of race and ethnicity, specifically being white and Latino. {... More verbose reasoning omitted}... To get the best results, the rewritten query should capture the essence of the question. ( ) Instead of direct question, maybe statement that reflects the core of the query. For example, "White individuals identifying as Latino" or "Intersection of race and ethnicity in Latino communities." ( ) But need to make sure its query that Contriever can handle effectively. Another angle is to use terms that are more likely to appear in Wikipedia articles. Terms like "ethnicity and race intersection," "Latino identity," or "multiracial identity." Maybe combining both terms in way that Contriever can retrieve documents discussing both aspects. )Wait, the original question is yes/no question, but ( Contriever might perform better with more descriptive query. Alternatively, using phrase thats more likely to appear in documents discussing both concepts. Maybe "Latino and white identity intersection" or "Can someone be both white and Latino." {... More verbose reasoning omitted}... After considering these options, the best approach is to ( ) rephrase the question into statement that clearly states the intersection, which is likely to be covered in relevant documents. So, the rewritten query might be "Can person be both white and Latino?" which is similar to the original but phrased as statement that Contriever can retrieve documents about. </think> <rewrite> Being white and Latino </rewrite> ( ) Result Metric: nDCG@10 of 0. BM25 is sparse retrieval algorithm, which in its nature prefers keyword-style retrieval. When the metadata (e.g., inverse document frequency) is computed on non-massive corpus, it is also sensitive to small-variants of phrasing and spelling (e.g., carryforward vs. carryforwards). We follow the optimal encoding setting of all retrieval systmes, such as using mean pooling for Contriever, and using the query instruction template for Qwen3-Embedding, etc. Retriever CovidQA DelucionQA EManual ExpertQA FinQA HAGRID HotpotQA MS Marco PubmedQA TAT-QA TechQA Avg. sections.title: 4 Results
sections.content: 4.1 Main Results RQ1.1: Can LLMs adapt their query formulation strategies to local retrievers via RL? We observe significant performance gains across diverse retrievers on the in-distribution RAGBench dataset  (Table 1)  . Adapting query formulation to the specific retrieverwhether through general prompts, specific instructions, or exploratory RLconsistently outperforms the baseline usage of the retriever alone, e.g., the BM25 retriever sees substantial jump in nDCG@10 when using the explore rewriting strategy compared to its raw baseline. This confirms that the model successfully learns distinct dialects suited for different backends. The learned query rewriter LLM agents also demonstrate strong generalization capabilities when applied to out-of-distribution and domainspecific environments. On the BEIR benchmark  (Table 2)  , our query rewriter improves the average performance of Contriever from 28.84 to 34.98 in zero-shot setting. In contrast, Gemini-2.5-Flash fails to provide useful rewritten queries even if under the specific prompts aligned with our rewriters, resulting in performance drop. Furthermore, results on the specialized FinAgentBench  (Table 3)  show universal improvements across all tested systemsincluding Contriever, BM25, and Qwen3-Embeddingproving that the learned structural adaptations remain effective even under significant domain shifts. Although our primary focus is on isolating the retrieval component from the full RAG pipeline, improved retrieval quality directly increases the likelihood that the gold document (d) is ranked highly, thereby improving downstream answer accuracy. In Appendix D, we empirically verify that these retrieval gains translate into improvements in end-to-end RAG performance. 4.2 Measuring the Discrepancy of Optimal Queries RQ1.2: Can strategies learned for one retriever transferable to others? To quantify the transferability of learned query formulation strategies across retrievers, we introduce Retrieval Environment MMD (RE-MMD), metric based on Maximum Mean Discrepancy that measures the distance between the distributions of optimal queries in source retrieval environment Esrc and target environment Etgt. We decompose distributional discrepancies into two complementary types of drift: Semantic Drift (Intent). Do the queries target different underlying information needs? We measure this using dense semantic encoder ϕsem Figure 4: Learning dynamics across different prompt methods and model sizes of query rewriter LLM. magnitude larger (often > 20.0), especially between sparse retrievers (e.g., BM25) and dense retrievers (e.g., Contriever). These results provide quantitative evidence that strategy transfer failures are primarily stylistic rather than intentional: the agent fails not because it seeks different information, but because it cannot effectively code-switch into the query formulation stylesuch as keyword density versus declarative phrasingrequired by the target retrieval environment. Overall, our findings suggest that query formulation policies optimized for one retriever generally do not transfer reliably to others. 4.3 Human Guidance in Prompt RQ2: How does the comprehensiveness of human guidance in prompts affect the learning of query formulation? Prompt design is known to substantially influence the learning dynamics of GRPO-based optimization. In this section, we investigate how different levels of human guidance embedded in prompts shape the learning behavior of query formulation policies. Specifically, we analyze learning dynamics under different prompt priors that vary in the amount of retriever-specific knowledge provided to the model. We conduct in-depth experiments in retrieval environments built with Contriever and bm25, which represent two retriever classes with distinct and human-interpretable optimal query behaviors. Figure 3: Measuring semantic and structural drifts of optimal queries across retrieval environments. Scores are our proposed RE-MMD values multiplied by 100. (e.g., MiniLM) that maps queries into an intent representation space. Structural Drift (Formulation). Do the queries differ in their surface-level formulation or dialect (e.g., keyword-based versus naturallanguage expressions)? We capture this using lexical representations ϕstruct. Formally, we define the squared RE-MMD distance in Reproducing Kernel Hilbert Space (RKHS) as: RE-MMD2(Esrc, Etgt) = (cid:13) (cid:13) 2 H, where µ = EqP [ϕ(q)] denotes (cid:13)µsrc µtgt (cid:13) the expected embedding of successful queries in corresponding environments. Details of the empirical estimation using kernel-based methods are in Appendix C. Results. Figure 3 reports the RE-MMD scores (scaled by 100) across retriever pairs. We observe clear contrast between the two types of drift. Semantic RE-MMD remains consistently low (typically < 2.0), indicating that the underlying information need is largely preserved across retrievers. In contrast, Structural RE-MMD is an order of Owing to its document span, document span contrastive training objective, Contriever is expected to perform best when the original query is transformed into hypothetical document resembling In contrast, the statistical the target document. nature of bm25 inherently favors concise, keywordoriented queries. We design three levels of prompt comprehensively (Figure 6 in Appendix provides the prompts in detail): General Prompts. We provide general query rewriting guidelines along with five candidate rewriting strategies. The query rewriter is instructed to reason over these options and select In this setting, the one strategy per rollout. rewriter is not informed of the underlying retriever and must infer its behavior solely through GRPO reward signals during training. Exploratory Prompts. We inform the query rewriter of the retriever being used and provide high-level description of how it was trained. The rewriter is encouraged to explore and infer an optimal query formulation strategy based on its understanding of the retrievers behavior. Specific Prompts. We explicitly inform the query rewriter of the retriever and prescribe concrete query formulation strategy provided from human experts, e.g., hypothetical document generation for Contriever and keyword-based rewriting for bm25. The rewriter is instructed to apply only this retriever-specific strategy. Figure 4 illustrates the learning dynamics for Contriever. The general prompt achieves reasonable initial performance but improves slowly, as the large space of rewriting choices makes exploration inefficient. The exploratory prompt yields the strongest initial performance and rapid improvement within the first 50 training steps. However, we observe that the model quickly converges to suboptimal policy that emphasizes keyword matching, which it prematurely identifies as effective for Contriever. This early policy collapse leads to convergence around the 200th step. In contrast, the specific prompt, which enforces hypothetical document generation, achieves the best final performance. Although this setting starts from relatively weaker initial pointdue to the rewriters random guesses about the structure of target documents in the black-box corpusit benefits from consistent guidance and gradually refines its strategy through GRPO rollouts, ultimately exhibiting higher performance and slower convergence. The conclusions differ for bm25, where the exploratory prompt outperforms the specific prompt, with both substantially outperforming the general prompt. Notably, even under exploratory prompts, the rewriter infers early in training that keywordstyle queries are optimal for bm25. This can be attributed to bm25 being well-established retrieval algorithm whose underlying principles are extensively discussed in the pretraining corpora of LLMs. In contrast, general prompts yield the poorest initial performance, as bm25s sensitivity to lexical choices causes many suboptimal rewrites to largely degrade early-stage rewards. Taken together, these seemingly contrasting results reveal an intuitive principle: when an LLM lacks sufficient prior knowledge or reasoning capability about retrievers optimal query strategy, explicit human expert guidance can effectively steer RL exploration and improve learning outcomes. Conversely, when the retrievers behavior is already well captured in the models pretraining knowledge, exploratory prompts are sufficientand can even outperform rigid, humanprescribed strategies. 4.4 Scaling of Model Size and Retrieval Steps RQ3.1: Can we enhance query formulation performance by scaling up model size? We examine the effect of LLM model size on query formulation performance. As shown in Figure 4, increasing the rewriter size to 14B enables the model to break through the performance plateaus observed for the 4B and 8B variants. This behavior highlights the role of increased model capacity and world knowledge in enabling more diverse and effective exploration, thereby mitigating premature policy collapse during RL training. Concretely, while prior knowledgeeither learned implicitly (e.g., the 8B model discovering that longer queries are beneficial under general prompts) or provided explicitly via human expert guidance (e.g., document-style queries for Contriever)suggests that longer or documentlike queries are optimal, the 14B model goes beyond these assumptions. Under exploratory prompts, it discovers distinct strategy that can be characterized as statement-style queries, which differ from both question-form and document-style rewrites. For example, given the original query Is it possible to be white and Latino?, question-style rewrite such as Can someone be both white and Figure 5: Training dynamics comparison of independent and branching rollout techniques Latino? achieves an nDCG@10 of 0.542, whereas concise statement-style rewriteBeing white and Latinosurprisingly attains an nDCG@10 of 0.965. This strategy emerges naturally from RL training with 14B model under exploratory prompts, as illustrated in Figure 2. We further observe that the diversity of strategy exploration scales with model size. At the early stages of training, larger models exhibit lower initial average rewards, reflecting broader exploration over wider range of candidate rewriting strategies, many of which are initially suboptimal. This increased exploratory behavior, however, ultimately enables the discovery of more effective query formulation policies. Overall, the model scaling results reinforce insights from the prompt ablation studies in the previous section. When base models lack sufficient internal knowledge about retriever-specific behaviors, human expertdriven prompts can effectively constrain the search space and guide learning toward strong policies. In contrast, larger and more capable models are able to discover non-obvious strategies that even human experts may not anticipate, allowing them to escape the local policy optima that constrain smaller models such as the 4B and 8B variants. RQ3.2: Can we enhance query formulation performance by scaling up retrieval steps? We observe that the 4 4 Strategy (branching) significantly outperforms the 16 1 Strategy (independent) in training stability. This performance gap stems from how the branching structure solves the credit assignment problem inherent in our shared reward function (Rtotal = 0.5Rturn1 + Rturn2). The branching strategy isolates the learning signal for the second turn. By generating = 4 continuations from fixed first-round outcome, the first-round reward becomes constant within that subgroup. When calculating the advantage for the second turn, this constant cancels out: turn2 (0.5C + R(i) A(i) turn2) siblings[0.5C + Rturn2]. (5) This effectively removes the noise from the first turn, allowing the model to receive precise gradient based solely on the quality of the second turn. Simultaneously, the 4 4 strategy stabilizes the first turn by acting as Monte Carlo estimator for its value. In the 16 1 strategy, the "future value" of first-round rewrite is based on single, noisy second-round sample. In contrast, the 44 strategy estimates the value of first-round action by adding its immediate reward to the average reward of the subsequent four second-round attempts that follow it. This tells the model: "Given this specific firstround attempt, here is the expected outcome if we try 4 different second-round attempts." This averaging reduces variance in the reward signal for the first turn, preventing the training collapse observed in the independent baseline.

sections.title: 5 Related Work
sections.content: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is standard approach for augmenting LLMs on tasks that exceed LLMs knowledge boundaries (Lewis et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2025a). For bridging the behavioral gaps between LLMs and retrievers, prior approaches mitigate semantic mismatches via query rewriting (Ma et al., 2023; Chan et al., 2024) or zero-shot expansion (Gao et al., 2023). While Reinforcement Learning has been used to align rewrites with generation goals (Nogueira and Cho, 2017; Ma et al., 2023; Jin et al., 2025), these methods typically assume uniform retrieval environment. On the information retrieval side, prior works show that different retrievers display distinct length preferences (Thakur et al., 2021; Xiao et al., 2023). In this work, we systematically analyze retrieverspecific query adaptation, addressing the structural drift where policies tailored for one backend fail to transfer to others. Agentic frameworks like Self-RAG (Asai et al., 2024) and Adaptive-RAG (Mallen et al., 2023) utilize dynamic, multi-turn retrieval but often face training instability in long-horizon trajectories (Shao et al., 2023). Complementing research on the retriever-generator preference gap (Ke et al., 2024), we introduce branching rollout technique that effectively stabilizes the optimization of multi-step query formulation strategies.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: We present the first systematic study on adapting LLM query formulation to specific retrievers via Reinforcement Learning. We demonstrate that optimal strategies are highly retriever-dependent and quantify this stylistic transfer failure as Structural Drift. Furthermore, we find that scaling model size enables the discovery of non-intuitive strategies that outperform expert heuristics. Finally, our proposed branching rollout technique stabilizes multi-turn training, paving the way for robust, truly retriever-aware RAG systems.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: First, our study focuses exclusively on text-only retrievers and text-based document collections, and does not consider multimodal retrievers (Xiao et al., 2025b; Chia et al., 2025; Xiao et al., 2025c) that can retrieve heterogeneous content such as images or audio. Extending our analysis to multimodal retrieval settings is an important direction for future work. Second, in our experiments on scaling the number of retrieval steps, we limit our evaluation to one-step and two-step retrieval. While these settings are sufficient to demonstrate the benefits of multi-step retrieval, it would be valuable to investigate learning dynamics and performance trends under longer retrieval trajectories in future studies.

sections.title: A RAGBench Details
sections.content: RAGBench (Friel et al., 2024) is large-scale RAG benchmark dataset which comprises more than 100,000 samples across diverse domains such as finance, law, and healthcare. Specifically, RAGBench collects extensive QA datasets, including CovidQA (Möller et al., 2020), PubMedQA (Jin et al., 2019), HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018), MS Marco (Bajaj et al., 2016), CUAD (Hendrycks et al., 2021), EManual (Nandy et al., 2021), TechQA (Castelli et al., 2020), FinQA (Chen et al., 2021), TAT-QA (Zhu et al., 2021), ExpertQA (Malaviya et al., 2024), and HAGRID (Kamalloo et al., 2023).

sections.title: B FinAgentBench and its Adaptation
sections.content: FinAgentBench (Choi et al., 2025) is financial information retrieval benchmark which contains over 18,000 samples curated by experts from SEC filings between 2023 and 2024. In the original benchmark, each sample is formulated as single large prompt that combines ranking instructions, financial question, and set of candidate document chunks, which are annotated with graded relevance labels. To adapt FinAgentBench to standard retrieval setting, we decompose each prompt into its constituent components and extract unique questions and document chunks across all samples. Because the same question may appear in multiple samples (e.g., when posed over different filings), and the same set of document chunks may be reused across samples (e.g., when multiple questions are asked about the same filing), we pool questions and chunks globally and retain only unique instances via exact matching, without any additional normalization or model-based processing. We then inherit relevance annotations from the original benchmark by mapping each question-chunk pair to its corresponding relevance label, thereby constructing conventional retrieval dataset consisting questions, documents, and graded relevance judgments. RE-MMD Empirical Estimation In Section 4.2, we defined RE-MMD as the distance between mean embeddings in highdimensional feature space. Directly computing the embedding ϕ(q) is often intractable. Therefore, we apply the kernel trick k(q, q) = ϕ(q), ϕ(q)H to estimate the distance empirically. Method Retrieval (nDCG@10) Accuracy EM Full RAG (w/ top 20 docs) no RAG + vanilla RAG + general rewrite w/ Gemini + (ours) retriever-specific rewrite Full RAG (w/ top 5 docs) no RAG + vanilla RAG + general rewrite w/ Gemini + (ours) retriever-specific rewrite - 41.01 39.16 52.44 - 41.01 39.16 52.44 25.3 41.4 42.3 46. 25.3 36.5 38.1 43.7 26.0 41.0 42.0 46.0 26.0 36.0 38.0 43.0 18.8 31.2 32.0 35.2 18.8 27.3 28.5 32.8 Table 4: Performance comparison of Full RAG setups using top 20 and top 5 retrieved documents. Given set of successful query trajectories Qsrc = {x1, ..., xn} from Esrc and Qtgt = {y1, ..., ym} from Etgt, we employ the unbiased estimator: RE-M D2 = 1 n(n 1) (cid:88) k(xi, xj) 1 m(m 1) i=j (cid:88) i=j 2 nm (cid:88) i,j k(xi, yj) + k(yi, yj) (6) To diagnose the nature of the drift, we utilize two distinct kernel functions: 1. Semantic Kernel. To measure intent drift, we use the Gaussian RBF kernel over dense embeddings (e.g., MiniLM): ksem(x, y) = exp (cid:18) ϕsem(x) ϕsem(y)2 2σ2 (cid:19) (7) This captures non-linear semantic similarities between queries. 2. Structural Kernel. To measure lexical/stylistic drift, we use Linear kernel over sparse lexical features (TF-IDF): kstruct(x, y) = ϕstruct(x), ϕstruct(y) (8) This is equivalent to the Euclidean distance of centroids in the lexical space, capturing explicit divergence in vocabulary usage and query formulation style.

sections.title: D Full RAG Performance
sections.content: To validate whether the performance gains observed in retriever-specific retrieval translate into improvements in the full-RAG pipeline, we conduct series of full-RAG experiments using HotpotQA as an example. Specifically, we adopt contriever as the retriever and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct as the generator, and compare our rewriter agent against vanilla RAG and general rewrites (nonretriever-specific) from Gemini-2.5-flash. We use the HotpotQA corpus from BEIR as the external knowledge base and the original HotpotQA questions as queries. Following standard RAG evaluation protocols, we report Accuracy, F1, and Exact Match (EM). As shown in Table 4, enhancements in nDCG@10 consistently translates to performance gains in the full RAG performance. Notably, these improvements remain stable across different numbers of retrieved documents, indicating the robustness of retriever-aware query rewriting. Zero-shot Multi-turn Retrieval Case

sections.title: Study
sections.content: We provide an illustrative example of multi-turn retrieval using our rewrite agent over four retrieval turns. Although the model is trained using only two-turn branching strategy in our experiments, it demonstrates the ability to generalize to longer multi-turn interactions in zero-shot manner. This suggests that the learned retrieval behaviors are not limited to the training horizon and can effectively extend to more complex interaction settings. Original Query: "What would be the effects on the us dollar if hypothetically it is no longer regarded as the world reserve currency?" 56.84. Rewrite Turn 3 (Further Enhancement): {omitted for brevity} Rewrite Turn 4 (Domain/Style Alignment): What are the financial and economic ramifications for the United States and global markets if the US dollar were no longer the dominant reserve currency, including impacts on foreign exchange reserves, corporate financial strategies, and the role of alternative currencies like the Chinese yuan? Analysis: After understanding the genre of available documents (corporate 10-K filings and financial disclosures) in this corpus, the agent adopts specific financial reporting terminology and be more specific about the domain, adapting to the environment style. The final rewrite enhances the nDCG@10 to 93.74, near perfect score.

sections.title: F Training Prompts
sections.content: Figure 6 describes the training prompts used in different settings of our experiments. Rewrite Turn 1 (Hypothetical Document Generation): The US dollars status as the worlds primary reserve currency has been cornerstone of global economic stability for decades. If the dollar were hypothetically no longer regarded as the world reserve currency, the implications would be profound and multifaceted. First, the international demand for the dollar would likely decline, leading to depreciation in its value. This depreciation could increase the cost of imports for the United States, contributing to inflation. Conversely, US exports might become more competitive, potentially boosting the economy in the short term. Central banks and governments around the world currently hold significant reserves of US dollars, using them for trade settlements, investments, and as safe asset. shift away from the dollar would require these entities to diversify their reserves, possibly increasing the role of other currencies such as the euro, the Chinese yuan, or the Japanese yen. This transition could lead to the emergence of new global financial centers and alter the balance of economic power. The United States would also face challenges in maintaining its influence over global financial institutions, such as the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and the World Bank, which have historically been dominated by US interests. reduced role of the dollar could diminish the USs ability to shape international economic policy, affecting everything from trade agreements to financial regulations. Moreover, the US governments capacity to borrow at low interest rates might be impacted, as the dollars status as safe haven could diminish. This could lead to higher borrowing costs for the US, affecting both the federal budget and private sector investments. In the long term, the global economy might become more fragmented, with regional currencies playing larger role in international trade, potentially leading to increased economic volatility. While the transition away from the dollar as the reserve currency would present challenges, it could also offer opportunities for the US to innovate in financial technology and strengthen its economic partnerships with other nations. The ultimate impact would depend on how smoothly the transition is managed and the policies implemented to support new global financial order. Analysis: The rewriter uses the hypothetical document generation technique in turn 1, but the terms are too general, yielding nDCG@10 of 0.0 in the first round. However, the rewriter is able to see the returned documents and learns how documents in the corpus look like. Rewrite Turn 2 (Entity Anchoring): What are the economic implications of de-dollarization and the rise of alternative reserve currencies, such as the Chinese yuan, on global financial systems and the United States? Analysis: Feedback from turn 1, although low scoring, exposes specific entities in the returned documents (omitted here). Thus, in the second round, the rewriter pivots to more specific directions, such as "de-dollarization", "Chinese yuan", and "global financial systems", enhancing the nDCG@10 to General Prompt: In order to retrieve supporting documents to answer the given question, you need to rewrite and extend given question into one new query. Remember that the rewritten query is not limited to simple question, but can be passage including multiple sentences that provides context for the input question. Here are some commonly used query rewriting methods, please select one appropriate method and use it to generate one new rewritten query: 1. Query Understanding: Extract core information from the query, and understand the core elements. For example, What was the federal alternative minimum tax credit carryforwards? can be phased as Federal, U.S. federal tax system; Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT), parallel tax system designed to ensure taxpayers pay minimum level of tax; Credit carryforwards, unused credits that can be carried forward to future years to offset regular tax liability;. 2. Semantic Rewriting: (1) Identify important entities and expand with related information. For example, Steve Jobs to Apple CEO Steve Jobs. (2) Add broader (hypernym) or more specific (hyponym) concepts. For example, husky to pet dog husky. (3) Change word order or phrasing to match common expressions in target documents. For example, make steak to steak recipe tutorial. 3. Query Decomposition: Break down complex query into multiple sub-queries for separate retrieval. For example, Which stock price is higher, Apple or Google? to <rewrite>what is the stock price of Apple?</rewrite><rewrite>what is the stock price of Google?</rewrite>. Also consider more complex queries that can benefit from breaking down into multi-hop queries. 4. General Rewriting: (1) Paraphrasing: rephrase the whole query if you think can possibly help the embedding model generate more accurate embeddings. For example, How to lose weight effectively? to What are some scientifically proven weight loss methods?. (2) Intent Refinement, infer the user true intent and rewrite the query accordingly. (3) Multi-hop Query Generation, generate indirectly related queries that help improve recall. For example, diabetic diet to fruits suitable for diabetics. 5. Hypothetical Document Generation: Generate hypothetical target document, in order to use this hypothetical document to retrieve target document. For example, (1) Given question asking about the law clause related to case, directly generate hypothetical clause for retrieval. (2) Given question about companys stock, directly generate hypothetical stock price report that looks alike the target document that contains the information. Before outputting the rewritten query, you need to think carefully about choosing which rewriting method, why choose this method, and how to use it. And please enclose your think process within <think> and </think>. Then the rewritten query must be enclosed between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Do not put the thinking process between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. For methods other than Query Decomposition, only one rewrite query is needed. If the question is rewritten as mutiple queries using one rewriting method (for example, when using Query Decomposition), please enclose each different query with separate <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Output can look like: <think> your think process </think><rewrite> rewritten query </rewrite>. User Question: Exploratory Prompt (Contriever): You are using retriever model to retrieve relevant documents to answer question. The retriever model you will be using is Contriever, which is trained using two spans of Wikipedia article as positive pairs. You will need to think about the best search strategy using Contriever and rewrite the original query into query that you think yields the best retrieval results for Contriever. Before outputting the rewritten query, you need to think carefully about what to write to look like target documents, enclose your think process within <think> and </think>. Then the rewritten query must be enclosed between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Do not put the thinking process between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Output can look like: <think> your think process </think><rewrite> rewritten query </rewrite>. User Question: Exploratory Prompt (bm25) You are using retriever system to retrieve relevant documents to answer question. The retriever system you will be using is BM25, which is sparse retrieval system. You will need to think about the best search strategy using BM25 (e.g., keyword-based) and rewrite the original query into query that you think yields the best retrieval results for BM25. Before outputting the rewritten query, you need to think carefully about what to write to yield the best retrieval results, enclose your think process within <think> and </think>. Then the rewritten query must be enclosed between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Do not put the thinking process between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Output can look like: <think> your think process </think><rewrite> rewritten query </rewrite>. User Question: Direct/Specific Prompt (Contriever) You are using retriever model to retrieve relevant documents to answer question. The retriever model you will be using is Contriever, which is trained on using two spans of Wikipedia article as positive pairs. Therefore, it works best for using document to retrieve document. You will be given user question, try using the "Hypothetical Document Generation" technique to generate hypothetical target document, which will be used to retrieve target similar documents. For example, (1) Given question asking about the law clause related to case, directly generate hypothetical clause for retrieval. (2) Given question about companys stock, directly generate hypothetical stock price report that looks alike the target document that contains the information. Before outputting the hypothetical document, you need to think carefully about what to write to look like target documents, enclose your think process within <think> and </think>. Then the hypothetical document must be enclosed between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Do not put the thinking process between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Output can look like: <think> your think process </think><rewrite> hypothetical document </rewrite>. User Question: Direct/Specific Prompt (bm25) You are query optimization assistant for BM25 retrieval system. Since BM25 is sparse, keyword-based model, it performs best with precise search terms rather than natural language. Your task is to rewrite the users original query into keywordoptimized version that maximizes retrieval performance. You must specifically optimize for term morphology and spelling variations (such as singular vs. plural forms or compound word handling). Because BM25 relies on exact token matching to calculate Inverse Document Frequency (IDF), using the most standard or canonical form of word is critical for aligning with the target corpus. Output the best possible search query for this system. Before outputting the rewritten query, you need to think carefully about what to write to yield the best retrieval results, enclose your think process within <think> and </think>. Then the rewritten query must be enclosed between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Do not put the thinking process between <rewrite> and </rewrite>. Output can look like: <think> your think process </think><rewrite> rewritten query </rewrite>. User Question: Figure 6: Training prompts used in our different settings.
